name: Account Planning Canvas
summary: A strategic framework for planning and growing key accounts. The canvas maps the account's business landscape, stakeholder relationships, competitive presence, growth opportunities, and action plan on a single page. It turns reactive account management into proactive strategic growth.
whenToUseIt: Use the Account Planning Canvas for your top 10-20% of accounts — the ones that generate the most revenue or have the highest growth potential. Complete it annually and refresh quarterly. It's essential before QBRs, during annual planning, when a key contact changes roles, or when a competitor starts circling. Don't try to create one for every account — focus on the ones worth the strategic investment.
steps: Account Overview — Document the account's business: industry, revenue, headcount, strategic priorities, recent news, technology stack. Understand their competitive landscape and market pressures. What's driving their business right now?

Stakeholder Map — Map every relevant person: decision-makers, champions, influencers, users, blockers. For each, note their role, what they care about, your relationship strength (strong/developing/none), and who else they influence. Identify gaps — who don't you know yet?

Current Footprint — What products/services do they currently use from you? What's the contract value, renewal date, and satisfaction level? Map the health of the relationship across teams and products.

Growth Opportunities — Where can you expand? New teams, new use cases, new products, additional seats, higher tiers. Prioritise by revenue potential, probability, and strategic fit. For each opportunity, identify the trigger event and the champion who'd sponsor it.

Competitive Landscape — Who else is selling to this account? What do they provide? Where are they stronger and where are you stronger? What's at risk of being displaced? What can you displace from them?

Action Plan — Turn insights into 90-day actions. Top 3 priorities: (1) Protect — what existing revenue is at risk? (2) Grow — which expansion opportunity will you pursue? (3) Deepen — which new stakeholder relationship will you build? Each action needs an owner, a deadline, and a success metric.
example:
Account: FastTrack Logistics (enterprise client, $180K ACV, 2-year customer)

Overview: Australian logistics company, 800 employees, $120M revenue. Currently focused on automating warehouse operations and expanding into last-mile delivery. Just raised $30M Series C.

Stakeholder Map: CTO (champion, strong relationship), COO (decision-maker, developing relationship), CFO (budget holder, no relationship — gap), VP Ops (influencer, strong), Head of IT (technical evaluator, strong).

Current Footprint: Warehouse management module ($120K ACV, renews in 5 months, satisfaction 8/10) and reporting dashboard ($60K ACV, renews in 8 months, satisfaction 7/10).

Growth Opportunities: (1) Last-mile delivery module — $80K ACV, high probability — CTO is already asking about it. (2) API integration tier upgrade — $25K ACV — Head of IT wants real-time webhooks. (3) New distribution centre rollout — could double seat count.

Action Plan: (1) Protect: Schedule renewal conversation with CTO 90 days out. (2) Grow: Demo last-mile module to CTO and COO by end of month. (3) Deepen: Get CTO to introduce us to CFO — prepare ROI deck for the meeting.
